paragraphs.body: Unraveling the Influence of Instagram Reels on Society Addictive social media such as TikTok, YouTube shorts, and Snapchat Spotlight, have been rapidly growing in popularity since the initial debut of short video scrolling in 2016. Not surprisingly, the rise of short scrolling videos galvanized fear that this new media genre triggers anxiety and depression in young people who are less able to protect themselves from bullying and trolling that many of the shorts posted on social media propagate. Instagram Reels, especially, has become a nursery for nonchalant disrespect for people and discriminatory behavior. It is the genres of clowning and self-clowning that I see particularly harmful. Clowning refers to someone making fun of someone else often without that person agreeing to be in the video. Self-clowning means that a performer makes fun of himself or herself, usually for clicks. Although not all videos on Instagram Reels are venomous, clowning and self-clowning videos can be venomous because they normalize gender and race-based discrimination. They do so not only via the videos, but also via the vituperative comments users post under these videos.

annotations.text: Although not all videos on Instagram Reels are venomous, clowning and self-clowning videos can be venomous because they normalize gender and race-based discrimination.
annotations.comment: This begins, immediately, with a compelling idea and problem. Nice friction :)

annotations.text: Unraveling the Influence of Instagram Reels on Society

o so not only via the videos, but also via the vituperative comments users post under these videos.

paragraphs.body: Wanting to be popular, accepted, and recognized is a normal human need. I, too, made fun of myself in lower school because it was an easy way to get friends. However, I do not think that it was a smart strategy. When a person accepts the role of the class clown, it is difficult to shed this role even when one changes and is no longer willing to act silly for laughs. Self-clowning also runs the risk that one is not taken seriously, or one is more easily bullied because he or she has already shown willingness to deprecate himself or herself. I am sure that I am not the only person who had this experience in school. I imagine I am also not alone in feeling triggered by some of clowning and self-clowning shorts I watch from time to time on Instagram Reels.

annotations.text: Wanting to be popular, accepted, and recognized is a normal human need. I, too, made fun of myself in lower school because it was an easy way to get friends.
annotations.comment: What if you developed this idea in narrative and with concrete and specific details. The moment would be much more meaningful and powerful if your reader felt like they were there with you, in the classroom and at school.

annotations.text: Self-clowning also runs the risk that one is not taken seriously, or one is more easily bullied because he or she has already shown willingness to deprecate himself or herself.

paragraphs.body: Journalists and scholars have discussed how difficult it is to keep bullying and trolling out of social media. In a New York Times article, Kevin Roose notes that executives of platforms like Instagram are aware that many of the platform’s features contribute to worsening teenagers’ mental health (2019). Often, however, platforms get stuck at the stage of defining bullying and agreeing what constitute unwanted behaviors. Similarly, the communication studies scholar, Tarleton Gillespie explains that the owners of social media platforms realize that they should invest more in content moderation to make their platforms appealing to their users and to advertisers (2021). However, hiring people to moderate content is expensive and this work is very demanding given that content moderators responsible for identifying abusive content often experience trauma. As an alternative, platform owners invest in developing AI to identify bullying and trolling images, videos, and comments, but AI has not yet evolved to the point that it could reliably perform this task.

annotations.comment: This is a sophisticated and well-written integration of several sources. This isn't easy to do, and you've done it very well!
annotations.context_left: os, and comments, but AI has not yet evolved to the point that it could reliably perform this task.

paragraphs.body: In what follows, I will use an example of clowning and another example of self-clowning to illustrate how these video practices normalize bullying and trolling behaviors. As mentioned earlier, the commenters are just as responsible as the producers of clowning and self-clowning for Instagram Reels into a toxic environment for young people. A good example is ‘untitled video’ by damianterantera. In this video, we see an example of a guy dancing with noticeable details of him having a facial structure similar to those with ‘down syndrome’ and him having a haircut called the ‘edgar cut.’ This is relevant, because once we open the comment section the very first comment we see is: “spedgar” from user named ‘username,’ which is a derogatory combination of the words ‘sped’ and edgar.’ And if you thought this was offensive, wait until you read the other comments. For example, user ‘username’ writes, “Music9/10, Dance10/10, Chromosome 78/76.” This comment is generally rating the performer’s choice of music and dance, while cunningly adding in a rating of 78/76 chromosomes, making fun of the idea that the performer may have more chromosomes than a ‘medically normal’ person. These two comments illustrate the extent of discriminatory and stigmatizing language in the comment section.

annotations.text: For example, user ‘username’ writes, “Music9/10, Dance10/10, Chromosome 78/76.”
annotations.comment: Yikes. Great example of your point, though.

I really like the way you include the video links. I wonder, though, if you could include video screen shots from the reel (maybe not so offensive moments from the reel) and then link the image? That would help you integrate images in a way that your audience would understand and appreciate.
annotations.context_left: comments illustrate the extent of discriminatory and stigmatizing language in the comment section.

paragraphs.body: And these commenters do not represent a minority. Hundreds of commenters, just like these two, normalize and amplify the position that it is fine to make fun of people who live with disabilities. In fact, this form of bullying is justified as these people are on board as they transform themselves into clown for the views. In their reddit comment describing Instagram Reels, Instagram reel comments are just embarrassing, user; StarryLightinMonsoon complains about the lack of humanity in the reels. According to StarryLightinMonsoon, “You could open up any Reel feed and see these prepubescent anti-shower teenage boys or misogynistic attention seeking teenage girls bullying someone for quite literally existing” (1). In making this comment, StarryLightinMonsoon laments the normalization of bullying in the world of Instagram Reels.

annotations.comment: Great quote burger templates! I wonder, though, if your explaining sentence could do a little more?

paragraphs.body: Of course, it is important to understand the difference between humor and trolling, but it is also important to understand that this line is very thin and often fuzzy. Did I laugh a little while reading the comments of the dancing video, yes. Would I post a vile comment like the hundreds of commenters? Absolutely not. In an untitled video by user ‘username’ we have a video that seems to teeter on the line between being funny and offensive. The video in question seems to contain a possibly inebriated young girl acting silly on camera. The comment section of this video contains multiple pictures or gifs intended to make fun of the girl. Whether these images are offensive and should be removed or whether they are just meant to be funny is up for debate. It further complicates this question that the girl in question seemed to be making fun of herself in the first place. Whether it is a healthy practice or not to clown/debase oneself for attention is an important question. One comment on the video from user named ‘username’ goes as follows: “1 day no fried chicken I’m seeing changes already.” This comment refers to the idea that she resembles a stereotypical black person of lighter skin color and, therefore, consumes large quantities of fried chicken. Another comment from user named ‘username’ has this to say: “Albinigga.” This comment I believe is a combination of the ‘n-word’ and the word ‘albino’ used to mock the girl for her appearance. Regardless of whether or not these comments are funny to me as an individual, it is obvious that they are funny to many other people. It’s important to discern the difference between funny and offensive.

annotations.text: Regardless of whether or not these comments are funny to me as an individual, it is obvious that they are funny to many other people.
annotations.comment: Aha! This is a moment of heat and friction. You're noticing something in the world, but what does this _reveal_ to you? Who is your audience, specifically? What do you want them to change/ do/feel/understand differently? In other words, what is the "so what?" of this discovery for your audience?
could you image link this one, too?

annotations.text: Of course, it is important to understand the difference between humor and trolling, but it is also important to understand that this line is very thin and often fuzzy.

paragraphs.body: The question to ask in relevance to this issue is whether or not it is okay to post comments like this on videos making fun of people, and whether or not it is okay to post videos clowning oneself in the first place. In a reddit post, I find it distasteful to pay people in poor communities to be clowns for our entertainment, user - username points to a website which allows for the buyer to pay for videos made by people in poorer communities who make fun of themselves. The famous influencer, Ace, puts it, “My immediate reaction is that this is distasteful and perhaps even dystopian but when you dig into it a bit more, you see that this is a charity that is trying to help these people who live in poor communities. Is it taking advantage of someone to pay them to beclown themselves for our entertainment? It feels like it is.” In other words, Ace believes that it is important to figure out if it is okay or it is justifiable to pay people to clown themselves or to reward people for debasing themselves for attention. This individual further argues that there may be coercion involved, which make the practice immoral, because the performer might not have acted on their own accord. They may post such controversial videos because they do not have other way of making a living. He concludes by claiming that he does not like this practice, but he wonders if it might have a positive effect as well. He, however, seems confused as to what exactly the positive side might be.

annotations.text: They may post such controversial videos because they do not have other way of making a living.
annotations.comment: Yes. Another important point, but you're still "noticing" a problem (and discussing it very, very well--this essay is highly sophisticated), but I'm not sure who your essay's audience is, yet.

paragraphs.body: All in all, a serious discussion is needed to consider in depth the negative social impact of Instagram Reels on youth and the practice of self-clowning, in particular. It is especially important to discuss the toxicity these videos spread. Asinine comments may entertain Internet users, but they can ruin someone's self-esteem. In retrospect, I wish I had never played the role of the class clown in lower school. I might have gained a few friends, but I am not sure they were the right friends and self-clowning hurt my self-esteem. But returning to Instagram Reels, in the first example video of the guy dancing, if he were to read those comments, he may end up really depressed or upset with himself for the way the comments describe him. On the other hand, many people might have gotten a good laugh out of said rude comments making their days better. In the second video of the girl goofing around, she would have most likely been unbothered by the rude comments or even laughing over them considering she was knowingly making a fool of herself in the first place. Likewise, the viewers or commenters might have also found the comments funny making themselves happier as well. Developing a better understanding of these practices as a whole is crucial to save the self-esteem of those unknowingly or knowingly being clowned on, or those who are clowning themselves.

annotations.text: All in all, a serious discussion is needed to consider in depth the negative social impact of Instagram Reels on youth and the practice of self-clowning,
annotations.comment: By whom? Aren't you having that discussion now? Also, how do Instagram Reels impact you, personally? What do you discover about yourself by watching these? Are they related to your experience as a child in school? Do they make you think about how you interact with people in the world now?
